name: before-after
description: Explicit state-change comparison — what was, what is.
purpose: Use to show the transformation produced by a change. Left = the prior state; right = the new state. Reads as a story, not a debate.

states.description: Exactly two list items. Authoring contract: a top-level bullet is the state label (Before / After); an indented bullet underneath carries the 1-2 sentence description.

skeleton:
<!-- _class: before-after -->

## What the change did.

- **Before.** How the system or process worked before the change, in one or two sentences.
- **After.** How the system or process works now, in one or two sentences.
sample:
<!-- _class: before-after -->

## What the manifest refactor produced.

- Before.
  - 35 layouts scattered across one 10,382-line lattice.css monolith. Per-layout rules grepped, not folder-located. No central metadata.
- After.
  - 45 components self-contained at lib/components, one folder each with manifest plus styles plus example plus README. Bundler concatenates per-component CSS; loader exposes the catalog via JSON.

whenToUse.title: Story of a change
whenToUse.body: Two states, one transformation — the audience reads left-to-right as a narrative, not a debate. Use after a decision has been made and shipped.

whenToUse.title: Concrete prior and new state
whenToUse.body: Both sides are factual descriptions of the system. The arrow between them is the change itself; the cards substantiate it.

whenToUse.title: Equal-density prose
whenToUse.body: Each card carries roughly the same length of body text. Lopsided density makes the After look heavier than the Before and the comparison breaks.

antiPatterns.title: Two competing options
antiPatterns.body: Use `compare-prose` or `split-compare` when both sides are alternatives under consideration. before-after is for a change that already happened.

antiPatterns.title: More than two states
antiPatterns.body: If there is a middle state or a sequence of changes, use `list-steps` to show the progression. before-after is binary by construction.

antiPatterns.title: Editorial labels on the cards
antiPatterns.body: The card label is always Before or After. Renaming them to Old way / New way defeats the universal grammar and breaks reader expectations.

related.name: compare-prose
related.when: two alternatives being weighed, not a transformation

related.name: split-compare
related.when: binary decision with a verdict bar

related.name: redline
related.when: the change is in verbatim text, not structural state

related.name: list-steps
related.when: the change has more than two phases
